segments.title: Grey Goose survives as a houseboat
segments.summary: Grey Goose is the surviving Steam Gun Boat, preserved in altered form after a postwar conversion to a houseboat in Kent.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by distinguishing survival from preservation: no Steam Gun Boat remains in its wartime form, although Grey Goose still exists. Built by J. Samuel White at Cowes, she was sold around 1957 and converted into a houseboat. He locates the surviving vessel at Hoo St Werburgh in the Medway district of Kent. Her altered condition explains both the rarity of the type and his hope that she might one day be restored to resemble Grey Goose in service.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Grey Goose, describes its postwar houseboat conversion, and gives its Medway location.

segments.title: A small remnant of a larger programme
segments.summary: Grey Fox's postwar fate introduces a Royal Navy Steam Gun Boat programme that produced seven completed boats and lost one.
segments.body: Grey Fox was offered for sale in October 1947 and, in Dr. Clarke's assessment, appears to have been scrapped. Her fate contrasts with Grey Goose's survival in converted form. The Royal Navy originally ordered many Steam Gun Boats, but only seven were completed and one was lost. The gap between the large intended programme and the small completed class helps explain why these boats are now an easily overlooked part of British coastal-force history.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Grey Fox, seven completed boats, and one loss.

segments.title: Steam Gun Boats as the costly coastal option
segments.summary: The Royal Navy paired Fairmiles with larger, costlier Steam Gun Boats in its response to the E-boat threat.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes the Fairmiles and Steam Gun Boats as two Royal Navy responses to the E-boat threat. Steam Gun Boats were the larger and more expensive option, but he emphasizes that the design was viable. Planned prefabrication was intended to support production on a much larger scale. That expansion did not happen, yet the boats that entered service performed well. He therefore presents them as the Second World War type that came closest to the older torpedo-boat-destroyer concept.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Fairmiles and Steam Gun Boats, explains the planned prefabrication, and assesses their service record.
